notes: Only groups A–L matches for A–L are present in the provided 72 predictions; however the JSON input included only groups A–L and only 6 matches per group for A–L (i.e., 12 groups x 6 = 72) is required—yet the appended array contains only groups A–L but appears truncated at group L (it ends at group L and includes A–L fully). Bracket placement for third-placed teams follows a closest-feasible mapping consistent with a typical 48-team/32-knockout template; exact FIFA 2026 third-place allocation table was approximated due to not being included in the prompt.

roundOf32.reasoning: Mexico’s host-context edge plus more reliable chance creation should overwhelm a Bosnia side that struggled to score in the group.
Canada’s transition threat and regional conditions offset Czech set-piece strength, and it profiles as a shootout-decided coin flip.
Brazil’s top-end talent and ball progression should pin Sweden back and limit their set-piece-heavy attacking routes.
Turkey’s stronger midfield control and finishing edge can punish Japan if Japan’s press leaves transition space.
Spain’s possession dominance and counterpressing should suffocate Egypt’s limited chance volume in open play.
Uruguay’s duel strength keeps it tight, but Germany’s depth and control tilt extra time in their favor.
Argentina’s game management and defensive stability should neutralize Scotland’s direct/set-piece routes while creating enough high-quality chances.
Austria’s intensity is real, but Portugal’s superior final-third quality and set-piece edge are decisive in knockout football.
France’s depth and athletic matchups across the front line should generate more clear chances than Algeria can trade.
Norway’s direct threat is dangerous, but Switzerland’s structure and game control make them slightly better in a low-event tie.
The USA’s pace can create moments, but England’s box defense and set-piece advantage should decide it.
Netherlands’ athleticism and chance creation ceiling is higher, and Croatia’s margin for error shrinks if they concede first.
South Korea’s pressing keeps it competitive, but Belgium’s superior final-third quality should convert key moments.
Morocco’s wide dynamics and pressing-to-transition patterns are well suited to breaking a compact Iranian block.
Paraguay can drag the game into low event states, but Ivory Coast’s athleticism and depth give them the edge in a shootout.
Colombia’s midfield balance and transition threat can exploit Ecuador’s aggressive pressing when the game opens up.

roundOf16.reasoning: A familiar matchup where Canada’s pace creates danger, but Mexico’s home support and control phases should tell late.
Turkey can threaten in transition, but Brazil’s ability to create multiple high-quality looks across 90 minutes is decisive.
Spain’s control keeps it level deep, but Germany’s depth and directness in the box swings extra time.
Two elite squads likely cancel out, and Argentina’s tournament game management plus penalty quality gives a slight edge.
Switzerland’s structure is strong, but France’s speed and depth in attack should create decisive separation.
Netherlands’ pressing and transition output can force England into lower-quality buildup and generate the higher xG share.
Morocco’s cohesion and defensive spacing make them awkward for Belgium, and their transition efficiency suits knockout variance.
Ivory Coast bring physicality, but Colombia’s midfield control and chance creation quality should produce the key goal.

quarterfinals.reasoning: Mexico’s host edge keeps it competitive, but Brazil’s attacking depth and individual shot-making is a decisive difference.
A tactical stalemate is likely, and Germany’s depth plus set-piece/penalty execution provides a thin edge.
Netherlands can press France into mistakes, but France’s pace in space and bench options can win it late.
Morocco’s structure is excellent, but Colombia’s ability to attack both in transition and settled play gives more scoring paths.

semifinals.reasoning: Germany’s control phases will be strong, but Brazil’s superior one-v-one attackers can decide the game in key moments.
Colombia are well balanced, but France’s athletic ceiling and depth across the front line can create separation without overcommitting.

thirdPlace.reasoning: Germany’s deeper rotation options and set-piece edge should decide a match that often becomes open and chance-rich.

final.reasoning: Two elite sides likely trade control, but France’s transition speed and bench quality can tip extra time.
